channel.title: Validation on FsFlow
channel.description: Recent content in Validation on FsFlow

item.title: Validation.apply
item.description:
Applies a validation-wrapped function to a validation-wrapped value.

Signature

Validation.apply validation value

Parameters

Name Type Description
validation Validation<(‘value -> ’next), ’error> The validation containing the function.
value Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation containing the value.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘next, ’error> The result of applying the function to the value, with accumulated errors.

Examples

 let fn = Validation.ok (fun x -> x + 1)
 let v = Validation.ok 5
 Validation.apply fn v // Validation (Ok 6)
val fn: obj
val v: obj

item.title: Validation.at
item.description:
Scopes a validation under the supplied path segments.

Signature

Validation.at path validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
path PathSegment list The path segments to apply to the validation.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to scope.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation nested under the given path.

Examples

 Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "fail") 
 |> Validation.at [PathSegment.Name "user"]

item.title: Validation.bind
item.description:
Sequences a validation-producing continuation.

Signature

Validation.bind binder validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
binder ‘value -> Validation<‘next, ’error> A function of type 'value -> Validation<'next, 'error>.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘next, ’error> The result of the binder or the original diagnostics.

Remarks

This is the monadic "bind" for validation. Note that this operation short-circuits and does not accumulate errors from the binder if the source has already failed. For accumulation, use map2 or the applicative and! syntax.

item.title: Validation.collect
item.description:
Collects a sequence of validations into a single validation of a list.

Signature

Validation.collect validations

Parameters

Name Type Description
validations Validation<‘value, ’error> seq A sequence of type seq<Validation<'value, 'error>>.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value list, ’error> A validation containing the list of values or accumulated diagnostics.

Remarks

This operation is applicative: it will collect errors from ALL items in the sequence.

Examples

 [Validation.ok 1; Validation.ok 2] |> Validation.collect // Validation (Ok [1; 2])

item.title: Validation.error
item.description:
Creates a failing validation result with the provided diagnostics.

Signature

Validation.error diagnostics

Parameters

Name Type Description
diagnostics Diagnostics<’error> The Diagnostics graph.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A failing Validation.

Examples

 let v = Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "Something went wrong")
val v: obj

item.title: Validation.fail
item.description:
Alias for error.

Signature

Validation.fail diagnostics

Parameters

Name Type Description
diagnostics Diagnostics<’error> The Diagnostics graph.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A failing Validation.

item.title: Validation.fromResult
item.description:
Lifts a standard Result into the Validation context.

Signature

Validation.fromResult result

Parameters

Name Type Description
result Result<‘value, ’error> The result to lift.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A Validation mirroring the result.

Remarks

If the result is an error, it is wrapped in a root-level Diagnostics graph.

Examples

 let v = Result.Ok 5 |> Validation.fromResult // Validation (Ok 5)
 let v2 = Result.Error "fail" |> Validation.fromResult // Validation (Error { Errors = ["fail"]; ... })
val v: obj
Multiple items
module Result

from Microsoft.FSharp.Core

——————–
type Result<'T,'TError> = | Ok of ResultValue: 'T | Error of ErrorValue: 'TError

item.title: Validation.ignore
item.description:
Maps a successful validation value to unit while preserving the diagnostics.

Signature

Validation.ignore validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<unit, ’error> A validation that keeps the original diagnostics and discards the success value.

Examples

 Validation.ok 5 |> Validation.ignore // Validation (Ok ())

item.title: Validation.index
item.description:
Prefixes a validation with an indexed branch.

Signature

Validation.index Validation.index validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
index int The branch index.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to scope.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation whose diagnostics are prefixed with Index index.

Examples

 Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "fail") 
 |> Validation.index 0

item.title: Validation.key
item.description:
Prefixes a validation with a keyed branch.

Signature

Validation.key Validation.key validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
key string The branch key.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to scope.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation whose diagnostics are prefixed with Key key.

Examples

 Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "fail") 
 |> Validation.key "id-123"

item.title: Validation.map
item.description:
Maps the successful value of a validation.

Signature

Validation.map mapper validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘value -> ’next A function of type 'value -> 'next.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source Validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘next, ’error> A validation with the transformed success value.

Examples

 Validation.ok 5 |> Validation.map (fun x -> x * 2) // Validation (Ok 10)

item.title: Validation.map2
item.description:
Combines two validations, accumulating errors if both fail.

Signature

Validation.map2 mapper left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘left -> ‘right -> ‘value A function of type 'left -> 'right -> 'value.
left Validation<‘left, ’error> The first validation.
right Validation<‘right, ’error> The second validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation with the combined result.

Remarks

This is the core applicative operation. If both left and right fail, their diagnostics graphs are merged.

Examples

 let v1 = Validation.ok 1
 let v2 = Validation.ok 2
 Validation.map2 (+) v1 v2 // Validation (Ok 3)
val v1: obj
val v2: obj

item.title: Validation.map3
item.description:
Combines three validations, accumulating errors when any input fails.

Signature

Validation.map3 mapper left middle right

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘left -> ‘middle -> ‘right -> ‘value A function of type 'left -> 'middle -> 'right -> 'value.
left Validation<‘left, ’error> The first validation.
middle Validation<‘middle, ’error> The second validation.
right Validation<‘right, ’error> The third validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation with the combined result.

Examples

 let v1 = Validation.ok 1
 let v2 = Validation.ok 2
 let v3 = Validation.ok 3
 Validation.map3 (fun x y z -> x + y + z) v1 v2 v3 // Validation (Ok 6)
val v1: obj
val v2: obj
val v3: obj

item.title: Validation.mapError
item.description:
Maps the error type of a validation graph.

Signature

Validation.mapError mapper validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘error -> ’nextError A function of type 'error -> 'nextError.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source Validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’nextError> A validation with transformed error values.

Examples

 validation |> Validation.mapError (fun e -> e.ToString())

item.title: Validation.merge
item.description:
Merges two validations into a validation of a tuple.

Signature

Validation.merge left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
left Validation<‘value, ’error> The first validation.
right Validation<‘next, ’error> The second validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<(‘value * ’next), ’error> A validation containing a tuple of the results.

Examples

 Validation.merge (Validation.ok 1) (Validation.ok "a") // Validation (Ok (1, "a"))

item.title: Validation.name
item.description:
Prefixes a validation with a named branch.

Signature

Validation.name Validation.name validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
name string The branch name.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to scope.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation whose diagnostics are prefixed with Name name.

Examples

 Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "fail") 
 |> Validation.name "email"

item.title: Validation.ok
item.description:
Creates a successful validation result.

Signature

Validation.ok value

Parameters

Name Type Description
value ‘value The success value of type 'value.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A successful Validation.

Examples

 let v = Validation.ok 5
val v: obj

item.title: Validation.orElse
item.description:
Falls back to another validation when the source validation fails.

Signature

Validation.orElse fallback validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
fallback Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to use when the source fails.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation when it succeeds, otherwise the fallback validation.

Remarks

This is a left-biased choice operator. If the source succeeds, the fallback is not used. If the source fails, the fallback validation is returned as-is.

item.title: Validation.orElseWith
item.description:
Computes a fallback validation from the source diagnostics when validation fails.

Signature

Validation.orElseWith fallback validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
fallback Diagnostics<’error> -> Validation<‘value, ’error> A function that turns the diagnostics into an alternate validation.
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation when it succeeds, otherwise the computed fallback validation.

Remarks

This is the lazy counterpart to orElse and is useful when the alternate branch depends on the accumulated diagnostics.

item.title: Validation.sequence
item.description:
Transforms a sequence of validations into a validation of a list.

Signature

Validation.sequence validations

Parameters

Name Type Description
validations Validation<‘value, ’error> seq The input sequence.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value list, ’error> A validation containing the list of values.

Examples

 [Validation.ok 1] |> Validation.sequence // Validation (Ok [1])

item.title: Validation.succeed
item.description:
Alias for ok.

Signature

Validation.succeed value

Parameters

Name Type Description
value ‘value The success value of type 'value.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A successful Validation.

item.title: Validation.toResult
item.description:
Converts a Validation into a standard Result.

Signature

Validation.toResult validation

Parameters

Name Type Description
validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The validation to convert.

Returns

Type Description
Result<‘value, Diagnostics<’error>> A result containing either the success value or the full diagnostics graph.

Examples

 let res = Validation.ok 5 |> Validation.toResult // Ok 5
val res: obj

item.title: Validation.traverseIndexed
item.description:
Maps a sequence into validations while prefixing each item with its index.

Signature

Validation.traverseIndexed binder values

Parameters

Name Type Description
binder int -> ‘source -> Validation<‘value, ’error> A function of type int -> 'source -> Validation<'value, 'error>.
values ‘source seq The input sequence.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value list, ’error> A validation containing the list of values or accumulated diagnostics.

Remarks

This is the indexed version of sequence. It is useful for list and array validation because each item can keep its own Index branch without the caller manually wrapping every item.

item.title: validate { }
item.description: This page shows the validate { } computation expression for writing validation logic with direct, sequential syntax. The builder is best for validation steps that read clearly as a block while still returning Validation<'value, 'error>. Use it when each bound step depends on earlier successful values. For independent sibling fields where you want maximum error accumulation, prefer Validation.map2, map3, apply, collect, or traverseIndexed so all branches are evaluated and all diagnostics are retained.

item.title: validate
item.description:
The accumulating validate { } computation expression.

Signature

validate

Returns

Type Description
ValidateBuilder A ValidateBuilder instance.

Remarks

Use this builder when you want to collect all validation failures instead of stopping at the first one.

Use and! when sibling validations should accumulate into the same diagnostics graph. Plain let! and do! are sequential: if the left side fails, the later step is not evaluated.

Check<'value> covers both value-preserving checks and gate checks. Use Check.orError to attach an application error, and Guard.Of / Guard.MapError when you want the same error-bound source shape to participate directly in validation.

item.title: Validation
item.description:
An accumulating validation result that keeps the structured diagnostics graph visible.

Signature

type Validation<'value, 'error>

Type Parameters

Name
value
error

Remarks

Unlike FSharpResult, this type is designed for applicative composition using and! in the validate { } builder, which merges errors instead of short-circuiting.

Examples

 let v1 = Validation.ok 5
 let v2 = Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "Error 1")
val v1: obj
val v2: obj
